channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Netherlands throws out disposable vapes ban plan
item.description: The Netherlands’ decision to drop its disposable vape ban plan shows the limits of prohibition-style nicotine policy. Concerns over enforcement, illicit trade and consumer behaviour appear to have outweighed the appeal of a simple ban. If adult smokers are to move away from cigarettes, regulation must keep lower-risk alternatives within legal, accountable channels. Better policy should target youth access and illegal products without removing safer options from the regulated market. (Published on June 17, 2026byclearingtheair.eu)

item.title: Tobacco Truth: The Cleveland Clinic Deceives Smokers
item.description: Smokers deserve accurate information about relative risk, especially from trusted medical institutions. Claims that vaping is “just as harmful” as smoking ignore the major difference between combustion and non-combustible nicotine delivery. When safer alternatives are framed as equivalent to cigarettes, smokers may be discouraged from switching to products that could reduce their exposure to toxicants. Public health communication should be clear about risk without confusing “not harmless” with “no less harmful”. (Published on June 17, 2026byblogspot.com)

item.title: Media Watch: The Trends Wire Muddies the Waters on Pouches
item.description: Numerous articles uncritically sensationalize WHO's stance on nicotine pouches, often funded by public health groups. The Trends Wire's alarming piece warns of child addiction worldwide. WHO data shows 40 million youth use tobacco, but nuances exist. The focus remains on traditional tobacco products like cigarettes, not pouches. The portrayal of nicotine pouches as a major concern is overblown, as they are a safer alternative. Addressing unregulated markets, most countries have existing consumer laws. The narrative against pouches overlooks their potential to reduce tobacco harms. Amidst the hype, understanding the true benefits of nicotine pouches is crucial. (Published on June 17, 2026bydaily-pouch.com)

item.title: Cannabis with Responsibility: Switzerland Takes a New Step Towards National Legalization
item.description: Switzerland’s move towards national cannabis legalisation reflects a more pragmatic approach to drug policy. Rather than relying on prohibition, the proposed model focuses on regulated access, consumer protection, quality control and prevention. The aim is not to present cannabis as risk-free, but to manage real-world use through oversight instead of leaving consumers to illicit markets. Evidence-based regulation should reduce harm, protect young people and bring existing demand into accountable systems. (Published on June 17, 2026bysechat.com.br)

item.title: The social impacts of prohibition #GFN26
item.description: Prohibition does not remove nicotine use — it reshapes where and how it happens. When safer products are banned or heavily restricted, consumers may be pushed towards illicit markets, unsafe supply chains and continued cigarette smoking. The social costs can include criminalisation, stigma, enforcement burdens and widening inequalities. Harm reduction offers a more practical path: reduce risk, preserve access to safer alternatives and regulate behaviour that already exists. (Published on June 17, 2026bygfn.tv)
